chapters.title: Everyone must learn history
chapters.text:
Many associate history with boring school textbooks and dusty research papers, but history helps us understand our origins and present selves.

Ukrainian historian Inna Kovalyshena combines friendly characters and their subjective views on facts from the history of Ukraine. While some pages are frightening, others provoke fascinating discussions.

Kovalyshena presents the history of her country through the story of friendship and teenage experiences. Four friends from different parts of Ukraine decide to travel through history. They collect memories of their ancestors, try to explain difficult moments to each other, and discuss their coming-of-age problems.

Learning history benefits those starting to study their region and those wanting to trace cause-and-effect relationships between the past and present.

History should not be a secret knowledge discovered after a certain age. On the contrary, adults and children need to understand the context of their country. Firstly, knowing our nation's past helps us feel included and responsible for our nation. Secondly, it protects the past from being forgotten. After all, we should preserve and learn from the experience of our ancestors.

Studying and understanding the history of one's country from childhood is a crucial aspect of shaping the civil society of the future.

Do not be afraid. We will not immediately dive into the subtlest nuances of past events. Instead, this summary will awaken curiosity about essential themes and raise issues we may contemplate for years. Besides, this adventure reveals Ukraine's diversity, complexity, and uniqueness.

So, let us begin our journey and explore this great country. Many historical events explain the innate strength and courage of Ukrainian men and women who still fight for their land in the Russian-Ukrainian war. Many tales unveil similar struggles for the independence of Ukraine across many centuries. After all, such efforts result in the most valuable reward, which is freedom.

chapters.title: The history of Ukraine is older than many believe
chapters.text:
Now, we are going back hundreds of millions of years to when the Carpathian Mountains were at the bottom of the ocean, and dinosaurs of the Riabininohadros weberi species roamed the Crimea peninsula. Closer to our time, about five million years ago, the Black Sea appeared. Later, mammoths, lions, and hyenas inhabited the territory of Ukraine.

Forty thousand years ago, the indigenous inhabitants of Europe, called Neanderthals, and African Homo sapiens lived here. Archaeologists found the oldest known housing structures in Mizyn and Mezhyritch, dating back to the 15th millennium BCE. There, the ancestors of modern Ukrainians hunted and made tools.

Around 5,000 BCE, the Trypillia culture flourished in Ukraine. Its representatives lived in settlements of up to 15 thousand inhabitants and made jewelry and ceramics with characteristic ornaments.

People engaged in agriculture in the north of Polissia and the west of the Carpathians. A southern region of grassland plains without trees except near rivers and lakes, called the steppe, allowed the nomads to domesticate cattle and horses. The east of modern Ukraine had copper mines four thousand years ago.

In the 5th and 6th centuries BCE, the state of Great Scythia formed to the north of the Black Sea. The Scythians replaced other nomads, the Cimmerians. The Greek historian Herodotus wrote about these places and their inhabitants. The Scythians primarily fought and traded with the Persians and Greeks, and they also made unique jewelry.

However, as often happened during those times, bloody-thirsty battles broke out, and the Sarmatians killed the Scythians. The Greeks wrote that Sarmatian women did not marry until they had killed three enemies.

During the Migration Period of the 3rd and 4th centuries, Slavs began to form tribal unions on the mainland of the future country.

Brothers Kiy, Shchek, Khoryv, and their sister Lybid founded Kyiv around the 5th century.

At the same time, new trade routes formed in Europe, including from the Varangians to the Greeks along the Dnipro River, from the warriors of Scandinavia in the north to Byzantium in the south. Kyiv became an important trade hub in the center of the route. Thus, the state of Kyivan Rus' gradually began to form.

During the reign of Princess Helga, Prince Ingvar, and their son Svyatoslav, local coins and a justice system appeared. Later, Svyatoslav's son Volodymyr adopted Christianity as the main religion of Rus' in 988.

Yaroslav the Wise, Volodymyr's son, created a list of laws called Rus' Truth. During his reign, many cathedrals were built that survive in Ukraine to this day, for example, St Elijah's Church in Chernihiv and Saint Sophia Cathedral in Kyiv.

Meanwhile, the Galicia-Volyn principality emerged in the west, where descendants of Volodymyr ruled. They often fought with the Mongol Empire but managed to build the city of Lviv and guarded the borders of the Kingdom of Ruthenia.

chapters.title: The fight for southern lands and Crimea
chapters.text:
During the reign of the Mongol nomads of the Golden Horde in 1240 to 1502, the lands of mainland Ukraine were divided between Poland, Hungary, and the Grand Duchy of Lithuania. At this time, wars unfolded in the Golden Horde, known as the Great Troubles. The armies of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, Rus', and the Grand Duchy of Tver fought the Golden Horde helmsman Mamai and Lithuanian Olgerd on one side, and the Timurids battled Muscovy on the other side. Tokhtamysh, who eventually killed Mamai, sought to take the throne.

Tokhtamysh's daughter, Dzhanike-Khanim, supported Hacı I Giray in founding the Crimean Khanate. He fought on the side of Tokhtamysh in the Great Troubles, whose troops included Tatars.

In 1441, Hacı I Giray founded a state on the territory of Crimea, the Northern Black Sea Coast, and the lands of the Northern Kuban, which became the Crimean Khanate.

In 1475, the newly created state recognized its dependence on the Ottoman Empire. And in 1569, the Grand Duchy of Lithuania and Poland united into the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. The Wild Field divided the two countries, a free steppe where people from both sides hunted and fished, and the Tatars searched for people to enslave.

Later, self-defense units began to gather there to resist the Tatar campaigns. These warriors were called Cossacks. In the second half of the 16th century, they created a fortified settlement named Zaporozhian Sich.

Soon, the Cossacks formed their own campaigns, particularly to the Crimean Khanate. They agreed to help the Crimeans fight against the Ottoman Empire, while the Khanate would assist the Cossacks in their struggle against the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth.

The Ukrainian revolution of 1648 to 1676 resulted in the Cossacks' state, the Hetmanship.

The Cossacks often organized raids. They viewed the rich as their enemies, since they came from less wealthy backgrounds.

Moreover, the laws of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth furthered this divide by putting Jews and Orthodox Christians on different sides of the barricades.

As Inna Kovalyshena has suggested, we can judge our ancestors, and we should, since we are their descendants, but we can also forgive them for not being better than their times.

Ukrainian hetmans signed agreements with other states to fight off enemies. For example, Bohdan Khmelnytskyi sought an alliance with the Ottoman Empire and the Muscovite Empire to oppose Poland. Ivan Mazepa, on the other hand, considered the Russian Empire his greatest enemy.

After the defeat of the Swedish and Mazepyn troops, the Russian emperor tried to abolish the position of hetman and destroy Zaporozhian Sich. When Peter I died, the Cossacks briefly restored their state until Catherine II's arrival, who destroyed Zaporozhian Sich in 1775. She also captured the Crimean Khanate three years later.

At the end of the 18th century, after the division of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, Austria seized the western part of Ukraine, and the Russian Empire occupied the rest, enslaving many Ukrainians. Unlike the serfdom of Kyivan Rus', where the peasants could utilize the land, the enslaved people, known as serfs, had no rights.

chapters.title: Culture is the foundation of a nation
chapters.text:
The existence of anything Ukrainian within the Russian Empire was seemingly impossible. However, Ivan Kotlyarevsky published the first three parts of his humorous poem Aeneid in 1798. It was a remake of Virgil with added nostalgia for the Hetmanate that became a rich gastronomic encyclopedia full of local characters.

And most importantly, it was written in Ukrainian.

Later, Taras Shevchenko's name rose to fame. The young artist and serf impressed the artistic community with his poems so significantly that a few artists bought him from his master for a large sum. Taras Shevchenko wrote about the struggle of the Ukrainian people for freedom from empires and masters, so his ideas resonated with many people.

In 1861, serfdom was abolished in Ukraine. However, in the Russian Empire, improvement in legislation was inevitably followed by a setback due to the carrot-and-stick approach.

In 1863, Russia issued the Valuiev circular, which partially forbade the publication of works in Ukrainian, or, as the Russians called it, Little Russian. In 1876, the Emsk Decree forbade using the Ukrainian language anywhere except for everyday life.

Both laws aimed to stop the development of Ukrainian culture and writing, but new names and titles appeared underground. At the beginning of the 19th century, the famous Ivan Franko, Lesya Ukrainka, Olga Kobylyanska, and many others continued writing. Ukrainian modernism was unstoppable.

During the First World War, a legion of Ukrainian snipers appeared as a volunteer unit in the Austro-Hungarian Empire. And in 1917, the revolution in Russia distracted the Empire, so that other nations had a chance to break free.

In March 1917, historian and politician Mykhailo Hrushevskyi headed the Ukrainian Central Council in Kyiv and later proclaimed the autonomy of the Ukrainian People's Republic (UPR). The Crimean People's Republic became the first Muslim republic in December, and it also granted voting rights to women. After the collapse of Austria-Hungary in 1918, the West Ukrainian People's Republic (WUPR) briefly appeared and united with the UPR in January 1919.

Meanwhile, a civil war raged in Russia between supporters of communism, the Bolsheviks, and the defenders of the monarchy, the White Guard. Communists believed utopia would prevail if everyone gave up private property. However, they sought to bring this ideology to all other nations regardless of their consent.

In 1921, the Bolsheviks seized the UPR and Crimea. Ukraine became the Ukrainian SSR. Despite their short existence, these forms of Ukrainian statehood laid the foundations of modern diplomacy for the future country.

For those curious about Ukrainian literature, the summary Ukrainian Fiction: A Starting Point offers a useful entry.

chapters.title: We must remember the 20th century, so history doesn't repeat itself
chapters.text:
After the emergence of the USSR, national cultures were briefly free, and the indigenization policy was supposed to strengthen local education and involve the nations in building socialism. However, the Soviets forced peasants to join collective farms and give up their land and livestock in 1928.

The leader of the Soviet Union reacted harshly to the peasants' protests and refusal to join collective farms.

The year 1932 marks the horrific policy of taking all food from the peasants, including grain. This period is called the Holodomor, the genocide of the Ukrainian people caused by an artificial famine.

Meanwhile, in the regions of Halychyna and Volyn, proactive students united in the Organization of Ukrainian Nationalists, an underground movement fighting for the independence of Ukraine. Eventually, the OUN split into two groups. One believed that Germany would help free them from the Soviet Union, while others compared the Nazi ideology to that of the Communists.

In 1941, the OUN declared it did not cooperate with the Nazis and would try to restore Ukraine's independence. The Gestapo immediately arrested the OUN's leader Stepan Bandera and his supporters. The remaining activists gathered in the Ukrainian Insurgent Army.

After the war, the Western allies disregarded the repressions in the Soviet Union. Besides, many such actions remained secret until the Union's collapse, despite the scale and brutality of the crimes of the Soviet regime being similar to those of the Nazis.

In May 1944, Joseph Stalin accused the indigenous people of Crimea of collaborating with the Nazis. Almost all Crimean Tatars were deported by freight trains to the republics of Central Asia over three days.

Many people died during forced displacement or due to the inhuman conditions of exile. This deportation was another act of genocide organized by the Soviet Union.

After the death of Joseph Stalin, the number of repressions decreased. The reign of his successor, Nikita Khrushchev, is called the thaw.

In the 1960s, a dissident movement appeared. It gathered many men and women in a fight for human rights. The lawyers, led by Levko Luk'yanenko, formed a political party attempting to gain independence. Regardless of the regime's weakening, the Soviets sent the activists to concentration camps.

The repressions soon returned, but this time, the regime opposers were sent to psychiatric hospitals, on account of various disorders. However, Ukrainians continued to form political parties and spread self-published materials and ideas of independence.

In January 1990, the People's Movement party initiated a live chain, uniting people from Ivano-Frankivsk and Lviv to Kyiv to celebrate the anniversary of the unification of WUPR and UPR.

In October, Ukrainian students staged Revolution on Granite, a successful two-week hunger strike against the new union treaty with the USSR.

chapters.title: Independence is a value we must fight for
chapters.text:
Ukraine adopted the Act of Proclamation of Independence on August 24, 1991. One of the authors was the lawyer Levko Luk'yanenko, who recently became free from exile.

In the 1990s, Crimean Tatars began to return to their homeland, despite Russia making several attempts to capture the peninsula long before the start of the Russian-Ukrainian war.

The first representatives of the Crimean Tatars appeared in the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine in 1998, namely Mustafa Dzhemiliev and Refat Chubarov. Later, they headed the Mejlis of the Crimean Tatar people, a representative body similar to a parliament.

Subsequently, in 2004, Ukraine elected its third president. Two made it to the finals, namely pro-Ukrainian candidate Viktor Yushchenko, who was head of the National Bank under the first president Leonid Kravchuk and the prime minister under Leonid Kuchma, and Viktor Yanukovych, a pro-Russian candidate.

The election results were rigged in favor of Yanukovych, and people protested peacefully in many cities, demanding to hold the vote again. The epicenter of the events was Independence Square in Kyiv, where the Orange Revolution began, named after the main color of Viktor Yushchenko's party.

Despite several reforms to restore historical memory and Ukrainian culture, the country was in an economic crisis during Yushchenko's presidency. Surprisingly, the following elected president was the one people protested against five years ago. Many hoped that Yanukovych would establish peaceful cooperation with Russia.

However, in 2013 Ukrainians demonstrated their determination to pursue European integration. People organized peaceful protests, and Independence Square became the epicenter again.

On the night of November 30, Berkut troops dispersed peaceful protesters by force and used brutality against many students. The next day, thousands spoke out against the police's violent actions in many Ukrainian cities, marking the beginning of Euromaidan, later called the Revolution of Dignity.

Until January, the scale of the revolution increased, and at the end of the month, the criminal authorities deployed snipers. At least 106 people, the Heavenly Hundred, died at the hands of Berkut and snipers.

At the end of February 2014, two crucial events occurred due to the Revolution of Dignity. Viktor Yanukovych fled the country and is still wanted, and Russian troops entered Crimea and seized the peninsula.

It is unclear whether Russian aggression was caused exclusively by the events of 2013 to 2014. Russia made several attempts to destroy Ukrainian statehood and began to gather troops in Crimea shortly before the revolution. Ukraine and its partners believed they could resolve the situation diplomatically, to no avail.

Immediately after the occupation of Crimea, Russian troops entered the east of Ukraine. Using propaganda, Russia tried to convince the world that the residents of the eastern regions were waiting for the Russian army during Ukraine's independence years. However, the Russian army met resistance from the Ukrainian citizens and military.

At the beginning of the war, the Ukrainian army was unprepared for the fight, lacking training and resources. European countries and the United States pushed for peace negotiations. However, Russia violated the agreements, so battles continued for eight years.

On February 24, 2022, Russia launched a full-scale invasion of Ukraine. This time, it was impossible for Russia to hide its crimes with a propaganda screen. The world sees the Russians' attack on Ukrainian statehood, civilian casualties, and shelling of peaceful cities.

As Inna Kovalyshena has put it, now we have no choice but to drive them out of our land, from all of our land.

This time, the Ukrainian military had combat experience and years of training. International partners quickly realized the Ukrainian army needed military assistance, not sympathy. The world understood that the freedom of Ukraine meant the liberty of at least the entire Europe. Now Ukrainian men and women are not alone in their struggle.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Inna Kovalyshena finished writing in the first months of the full-scale Russian invasion, but the war had lasted for nine years at that point. Her heroes and heroines travel by evacuation train, escaping constant shelling. Despite its short length, this tale demonstrates that Russian aggression against Ukrainian identity has lasted much longer.

The characters come from different regions of Ukraine, from east to west and south to north. Their ancestors have different experiences, which could have become a reason for hostility hundreds of years ago. However, today Dmytryk, Alia, Sashka, and Yasya are united by the desire to explore their country's history. They have gradually understood that, in the end, their ancestors came together due to a shared passion, which is the independence of Ukraine.

History can help people better understand their identity. It can teach acceptance of otherness and understanding of other experiences. This mutual understanding helped Ukraine finally win its independence.

Of course, covering all aspects of forming an entire state would require more than one text. But even such a concise retelling of Ukrainian history shows how challenging the path to independence was. Ukrainians carried this purpose through generations, regardless of the difficulties.

With this great purpose, Ukraine will continue holding on to its sovereignty. The memory of the past helps Ukrainians fight for the future.

Try this. Explore books by Serhii Plokhy, Anne Applebaum, Timothy Snyder, and Slavenka Drakulić. Share your knowledge of Ukrainian history with your loved ones to help informational resistance to Russian aggression. Look for information from several verified sources to avoid becoming a victim of propaganda. Research the history of your country and talk to eyewitnesses of the events where possible.

Read it till the end?
